timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Fall of Northern Kingdom

sections.header: Verses 1–22 — Amaziah’s Reign; War with Israel; Jerusalem Breached

panels.ctx: Amaziah of Judah does right “but not as David had done” — the qualified verdict. He defeats Edom, then unwisely challenges Israel’s Jehoash. Jehoash replies with a parable: “A thistle in Lebanon sent a message to a cedar: ‘Give your daughter to my son in marriage.’ Then a wild beast trampled the thistle.” Amaziah ignores the warning. Israel defeats Judah, breaks down 180 metres of Jerusalem’s wall, and plunders the temple. A Judahite king’s overconfidence produces the first breach of Jerusalem since David captured it.

cross.note: David captured Jerusalem and made it his capital. The breach of the wall under Amaziah reverses David’s achievement.
“Pride goes before destruction, and a haughty spirit before a fall.” Amaziah’s challenge to Israel is pride incarnate.

notes.note: The thistle parable teaches that self-knowledge is essential to wisdom. Those who overestimate their own strength court destruction. Humility is not weakness but accurate self-assessment.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Sela (literally “rock”) may be Petra or another Edomite fortress. The renaming to Joktheel is a standard ANE practice of asserting sovereignty through toponymic change.

wiseman.source: Donald J. Wiseman, 1 and 2 Kings, Tyndale OT Commentary (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The Valley of Salt (Wadi el-Milh, south of the Dead Sea) was a recurring battlefield between Judah and Edom. David fought there (2 Sam 8:13); Amaziah’s victory echoes the earlier campaign.

provan.source: Iain W. Provan, 1 and 2 Kings, New International Biblical Commentary (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Amaziah’s defeat by Israel demonstrates that military victory over one enemy (Edom) does not guarantee invincibility. Success in one arena produces dangerous overconfidence in another.

sections.header: Verses 23–29 — Jeroboam II: Prosperity Without Righteousness

panels.ctx: Jeroboam II reigns 41 years and restores Israel’s borders from Lebo Hamath to the Dead Sea — the greatest territorial extent since Solomon. The narrator mentions Jonah son of Amittai (v.25) as the prophet who predicted this expansion. But the treatment is shockingly brief: the most prosperous king since Solomon gets seven verses. The narrator has no interest in political success that isn’t accompanied by covenant faithfulness. Amos and Hosea prophesy during this reign, condemning the wealth and injustice that Jeroboam’s prosperity enables.

cross.note: Amos condemns the luxury of Jeroboam II’s elite: ivory beds, choice meats, idle music — while the poor are trampled. Prosperity without justice is judgment in disguise.
Jonah son of Amittai — the same prophet mentioned here — is sent to Nineveh. His Israelite nationalism (he wants Nineveh destroyed) fits the context of Jeroboam’s expansionist reign.

notes.note: God’s compassion for Israel during Jeroboam II’s reign demonstrates that divine mercy is not conditioned on human merit. God sends relief to a wicked nation not because they deserve it but because he is gracious.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Lebo Hamath (modern Labweh at the watershed between the Orontes and Litani rivers) marks the northern boundary of Israel’s ideal territory. Its recovery under Jeroboam II is confirmed by the general geopolitical situation: Assyria’s temporary weakness allowed regional powers to expand.

wiseman.source: Donald J. Wiseman, 1 and 2 Kings, Tyndale OT Commentary (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The borders “from Lebo Hamath to the Dead Sea” match the ideal boundaries described in Num 34:7–12. Jeroboam II’s achievement represents the closest any northern king comes to the Davidic-Solomonic ideal — but without its theological foundation.

provan.source: Iain W. Provan, 1 and 2 Kings, New International Biblical Commentary (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Seven verses for a 41-year reign is the narrator’s most eloquent commentary: political success without covenant faithfulness is theologically irrelevant. The Deuteronomistic historian is not writing political history but theological history.

ppl.name: Israel
ppl.text: He defeats Edom, then unwisely challenges Israel’s Jehoash…

ppl.name: Judah
ppl.text: Amaziah of Judah does right “but not as David had done” — the qualified verdict…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVYet he did not put the children of the assassins to death, in accordance with what is written in the Book of the Law of Moses where the Lord commanded: \u201cParents are not to be put to death for their children, nor children put to death for their parents; each will die for their own sin.\u201dESVBut he did not put to death the children of the murderers, according to what is written in the Book of the Law of Moses, where the LORD commanded, \u201cFathers shall not be put to death because of their children, nor shall children be put to death because of their fathers. But each one shall die for his own sin.\u201dNIVAfter the kingdom was firmly in his grasp, he executed the officials who had murdered his father the king.ESVAnd as soon as the royal power was firmly in his hand, he struck down his servants who had struck down the king his father.
